entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): World History: Grades 10-12, Ancient History: Grades 10-12

About Ancient History: Grades 10-12
Drawing on material culture, primary sources, and wider overviews, this course delves into the time period from 100 BC to 350 AD.

About World History: Grades 10-12
Read about world history from 1800-1900. View images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries.

About U.S. History: Grades 10-12
Read about U.S. history from 1800-1900 through biographies, primary source documents, creative nonfiction, and history spines. View images and reference atlases and maps to put the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Ancient History: Grades 10-12
Inquisitive 9th graders could enjoy this course, but the main texts have an advanced reading level, so students would likely want to move at a slower pace.

blocks.text:
• Take a second to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

• If the previous passage is difficult to remember, try looking back at any chapter/section headings or illustrations or reading the first paragraph to jog the memory.

• Read the selection assigned OR read for the number of minutes allocated, leaving time for narration and mapwork. Use whichever guide (i.e., pages or minutes) works best for you. You want to read enough to be interesting, but not so much that you feel overwhelmed.

• If a book is challenging for you as a student, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently for a minute every now and then to make sure you are tracking. You might also want to read aloud to yourself. Or you might want to listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along with it. 

• If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! 

• Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

• Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. 

• To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

• After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, and other things you've read, watched, or seen in the past or present. 

• Share any ideas that are particularly interesting to you with your fellow classmates, teachers, parents, friends, or others. You might even want to do a bit more reading or research on something on your own time in the afternoon or evenings.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

Those educating at home might pick one or two history lessons to read aloud with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship, and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete themselves silently and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China
➜ INTRO
One of the most important actions one can take when creating a new nation is establishing trade with other countries. As a newly founded nation, that is exactly what the United States of America set out to do. The United States traded for things like silk, gold and tea while other nations traded for everything from sea otter and seal pelts, to sandalwood, to opium, to cotton, and tortoiseshell. As the United States navigated new waters--both literally and figuratively--they will come into contact with a mighty trade nation that has a lot to offer them including the ever-important tea. However, China had it's own rules and regulations as well as it's own system of justice that was very different to the United States.
As you read through the chapter, I invite you to ponder what it must have been like to navigate these new waters and to meet new peoples whose way of life was so different from yours? What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? You will reflect on these questions after the reading.
➜ ANNOTATE
When America First Met China is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return too. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.
➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.146-161 - Stop at “Bêche-De-Mer,”
Narrate silently. 
Answer the following questions in 3 paragraphs
What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? What things made you uncomfortable and why?

lessons.title: 40m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Lesson 1
⍞ Materials: American History: Volume 1

➜ INTRO
Starting this year, we will be reading through Thomas Kidd’s American History: Volume 1 & 2. We will begin in chapter seven as this is the chapter opening up the 19th century. The United States of America has established its independence from Britain but must now recover from war and navigate the land, the laws, and find new leaders. Over the course of the next hundred years, we will walk alongside people as they navigate the formation of what would become a fifty-state nation. If you are interested and have the time, you are invited to peruse the first few chapters of the textbook if you would like a quick review of where we have left off.

➜ ANNOTATE
American History is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ 
Introduction. p.1-2
Ch.7 p.137-148 - stop at “the second great awakening”

➜ NARRATE
1. Narrate silently. 
2. In one paragraph summarize the political climate in the early 1800s seen in the text. Who are the people you are encountering? What are the popular movements of the day? What themes are popping up in the reading that Kidd mentioned in his introduction.
lessons.teacherNotes:
STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

lessons.body:
Welcome to the Empire
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires

➜ INTRO 
What comes to your mind when you hear the word "empire?" Historians disagree on many things including times and dates, translations, and terminology. One of the terms that is debated is the word "empire" and its meaning. As you read through the introduction to Ancient Empires, what definition do you agree with? Why do you agree with it? You're invited to answer these questions aloud or write them down.

➜ READ
Introduction: "What is an (Ancient) Empire?" p.1-5 stop at "Michael Mann..."
If you have already read this resource, just review the Introduction section assigned here.

➜ NARRATE
Narrate silently.

lessons.body:
Intro to Primary Sources
⍞ Materials: American Horizons: Volume 1

➜ INTRO
Primary Sources are one of the ways that you can encounter the real lives of people in history. American Horizons is a collection of visual and written documents that show how people were thinking and feeling throughout the 1800s. There are questions at the end of each reading section and not all of them are  assigned but you are invited to ponder them as you read through the different documents. Use this reading to place yourself in the time and place in history. 

➜ READ 
p.XI, 73-74, 82-83

➜ NARRATE
1. Narrate silently.
2. Answer questions 1-3 on p. 83 in three paragraphs.
3. Use the empty "primary source" template and fill in the information for all three of the sources. Use the filled in "Primary Source" template to guide you into asking the right questions about authorship, genre, audience, historical context, bias and framing, stylistic elements. You can also refer back to P.XI in American Horizons: Volume 1.
				∞ PDF Link: Primary Source Template (Filled) 
        ∞ PDF Link:  Primary Source Template (Empty)

➜ PONDER
What are some possible visual and written documents that will be used in schools in the years to come? How do you think technology will impact what primary sources are available to the next generation of historians?

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China
➜ INTRO
In this chapter you will encounter a woman named Harriet Low who has recounted many of her adventures in journals. Harriet Low talked much about her time in an area of China that was a forbidden to women and her journals are insightful to anyone interested in history. Her journals are a primary source. 
Throughout the coming chapters, Eric Jay Dolin will rely heavily upon primary sources to help his story telling, while keeping it factual and correct. As you read through the next section, pay attention to the documents Eric Jay Dolin is mentioning.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China
➜ INTRO
In Chapter 7, Eric Jay Dolin will explore China through America's eyes. Specifically, he will explore how the nation came to be perceived through all different means. People spoke about subjects such as the rampant poverty, their treatment of women, the things they ate and drank and the violence of their justice system which created a picture of the China and it's people which influenced the perception of the nation.
➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.192-208
Narrate silently. 
In three paragraphs, take time to reflect on what influenced the perception of the Chinese people. 
➜ PONDER 
How is your nation perceived? What are some nations that you have perceived and how have you perceived them?

lessons.title: 40m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Lesson 7
⍞ Materials: American History: Volume 1

➜ INTRO
In his book, Why Study History, author and Historian John Fea writes, "Indeed, anyone who studies the past realizes that there are no heroes in history. While people may perform heroic acts, all humans are tainted by sin and are susceptible to acting in ways that preference themselves over others and God. Historians understand, perhaps better than most, the reality of the pain, suffering, injustice, anger, and vice brought on by sin. In other words, they understand the tragic dimensions of life."
As you enter into this next chapter, you will encounter more and more people tainted by sin. As the struggle over slavery begins in the United States of America there will be people who inspire and people who reveal the depravity of humanity in their actions towards others. I invite you to think like a historian and enter into the pain, suffering, injustice, anger, and vice brought on by sin that you witness within the next pages. It's important to remember that while John Fea's earlier words are important and true, what he adds later is, "A belief in the reality of sin should provide us with a healthy skepticism about movements in the past committed to utopian ends, unlimited progress, or idealistic solutions to the problems of this world. This, of course, does not mean that we should stop working toward these ends, but history certainly teaches us that we live in a broken world that will not be completely fixed on this side of eternity. Similarly, a belief in depravity helps us to better explain the human condition—the restlessness, the search for meaning, and the prideful ambition that has defined much of the past, especially in the modern era. Augustine was quite correct when he opened his Confessions with the famous words, “Our hearts are restless until they rest in you.”

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.169-184 from “The Worlds of Slave and Masters” to end of chapter.
Narrate silently. 

➜ VIEW
Take a minute and look at the bibliography at the end of the chapter. Why do you think these sources were chosen? Do they look trustworthy? What sources stand out to you? What sources are you interested in reading further? 

➜ PONDER
What are some of the things that surprised you in the reading?  How do you see the church reacting to what is going on around them?

lessons.title: 40m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Lesson 9
⍞ Materials: American History: Volume 1

➜ INTRO
Have you ever watched a western? Perhaps there is a favorite book you’ve read set in the time of cowboys and early American exploration? The chapter you are about to read concerns the American West. As you will see, the American West is more complicated than what we encounter in books, television and movies. There are battles that change the trajectory of many peoples lives as well as political choices that fuel a later war. There is constant movement from every people-group as they hope to find a new home. I invite you to ponder a couple questions as you read through the chapter. What are some things happening in the reading that shocked you? What was new? What has changed based on your reading? What have you learned that isn't consistent with what you already know?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9 p.185-193- to “The Mexican War”
As you finish each section (headed by a red-lettered title), stop, and narrate either with a summary statement in writing or orally to yourself or others.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ NOTE
Take both lessons this week to explore the ORBIS site.

➜ VIEW
∞ Link: Peutinger Table (Roman Road Map, 13th c. copy based on a possible Roman original)
Be sure to watch the YouTube clip to see the complete scan from west to east! A more detailed, interactive site where you can see individual sections is found on Euratlas. 
∞ Link: Euratlas

➜ VIEW
 ∞ Link: ORBIS

lessons.title: 30m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Lesson 14
⍞ Materials: American History: Volume 1
 
➜ INTRO
As you read Chapter 10 you will encounter many topics. The first being education in America in the 1800s. From higher education like Harvard and Dartmouth College, all the way to education for young boys and girls. The second will be science and faith and the intertwining of these two social spheres. There will be many events that spark conversation about science and faith but the most important will be the publishing of Charles Darwin’s Origin of Species in 1859. As you will read, christians and non-christians will be in discussion over the topic of evolution and the Genesis one narrative. The third topic you will encounter is the Bible and Slavery. This section will focus on the reaction from Christians to slavery and to the Bible. Is slavery something you find in the Bible? Yes Does that mean slavery is acceptable? This question along with many others will become the topic of conversation for North and South for years and years. 

➜ READ AND NARRATE
Ch.10 p.203-212 – To “Theological Liberals and the Transcendentalists” 
As you finish each section (headed by a red-lettered title), stop, and narrate either with a summary statement in writing or orally to yourself or others.

➜ WRITE
Begin compiling a list of all the things you’ve seen in your reading which contributed to the Civil War.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China
➜ INTRO
How long do we wait for our packages? Perhaps if we have fast shipping we'll only have to wait one or two days for our order to be at our doorstep. In the 1800s, it would take weeks for one ship to get to the other side of the world and pick up the items and then weeks to return. So, as one can imagine, there was a need to be the fastest ship out there in order to get the items back before anyone else. Many people took on the challenge of building these ships. Think on how shipping and trade has changed over the years as you read through this chapter?

lessons.title: 30m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Lesson 16
⍞ Materials: The Narrative of Lunsford Lane

➜ INTRO
The fight for freedom for many slaves did not begin and end with the civil war. There had been continuous struggles for freedom prior to 1861. Men and women escaped the horrors of slavery by many avenues. The Narrative of Lunsford Lane explores an avenue that isn’t talked about often: purchasing one’s freedom.
Lunsford Lane is an autobiography written and published in 1842. His story is one of bravery, perseverance, family, and struggle. He encounters the worst of humanity, but he also encounters people who will help him take on entire systems of injustice.
As Christians, we grieve as we read these stories, where those made in the image of God are dehumanized and abused. Nevertheless, hearing these stories of suffering and overcoming are essential. We pray that your heart remains soft and that you read and process these stories with a longing for justice and an empathy for those who have gone through what no man or woman should go through.

➜ READ
LL pg. 6-24 - stop at "I had endured..."

➜ NARRATE
Narrate silently. 

➜ WRITE
Identify the people you meet in this chapter and their character. What did you learn about Lunsford Lane's history?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Erebus
➜ INTRO
What happens when nations finds themselves with war vessels, but no wars? For 1800s Britain, the answer was to send its ships on exploration missions. The Erebus was just one of the ships that made the transition from military vessel to a vessel used for research and exploration. The captains and crew of the vessel changed over the many years it spent on the seas.
In Erebus, you will read a book about a legendary ship. However, there is another benefit to Michael Palin’s book which makes it a helpful read as well as an enjoyable one. Erebus does not just share the story of the ship itself, but of the world in which this ship sailed. As you read through Erebus, take note of the names being mentioned in each chapter.
I also invite you to adventure with the people on Erebus and on the HMS terror. Pay attention to the letters and journals that are being mentioned and remember that these were real people who experienced a world very unlike our own. And yet, you’ll also see a world that is very familiar too.
➜ ANNOTATE
Erebus is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.
➜ READ
Ch. 1
➜ NARRATE
Experiment with different ways of narration. Write or discuss the world figures and the events that you’ve read about in chapter one of Erebus. Who has appeared in the chapter? Why are they important? What is the historical setting of the book?

lessons.title: 40m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Lesson 25
⍞ Materials:  American History: Volume 1, American Horizons Volume 1

➜ INTRO
John Fea, in his book Why Study History challenges the reader with the line, "Historians also study the past in context." In reading about the subject of history there is a challenge in looking at the past with the eyes of someone living in the 21st century. To quote Fea again, "Any event from the past should be understood in light of the circumstances, settings, or belief systems in which it occurred." I invite you to ponder what circumstances, settings and belief systems are being carried by those you are reading about in both American History and American Horizons.  How do they differ from your own? What do you know now that they did not know at the time? And what knowledge did they have that you might be lacking?

American Horizons pg. 111-112, 113-114

lessons.title: 30m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Lesson 26
⍞ Materials: They Fought Like Demons 

➜ INTRO
The Civil War lasted for four years. Over those four years there are many letters from husbands and fathers and sons to their loved ones. But if one looks deeper into the Military Archives—like Deanne Blanton and Lauren Cook did—they will find peculiar letters to family members from people like Francis Clayton and Albert Cashier. The peculiarity comes from the fact that these men did not really exist. Instead, they were alias that women took on to be able to join the fight—on both sides. These women masquerade as men, became spies, doctors, and nurses, and shared in the fate of their fellow soldiers. Their stories show that women were not just within the home but were actively participating and dying for their country and their beliefs.
They Fought Like Demons is a book about a war. Thus, this book has tales of violence and death. But it also tells the stories of women whose love for their families, their beliefs and their country drove them to hide their identity and brave the battlefield. 

➜ READ AND NARRATE 
p.1-8

lessons.teacherNotes:
• TERMINOLOGY
The phrase "Away with the atheists" appears several times in some translations of the text but had a different meaning in Roman times. Roman persecutors called the Christians "atheists" because they refused to worship the Roman gods. Later, Polycarp says, "Away with the atheists" in irony, referring to the unbelieving Romans in the stadium.

lessons.title: 30m U.S. History: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 50
lessons.body:
Lessons 50
⍞ Materials: I am a Man

➜ INTRO
The story you are about to read is riddled with violence. I am a Man is about the forced removal of tribe called the Ponca. They were given a plot of land to live and cultivate, but years later found themselves thrown off that land and they were made to travel to a harsh climate that had none of the benefits of their previous home. The violence that you will encounter in today's reading is included to give us a better sense of the realities of these men, women and children and to help us sympathizes snd empathize with their plight. 
As you read through this book, you will be grieved and we grieve with you. We grieve the poor treatment of those who are made in God’s image. We grieve the loss of life because of the actions of our fellow man. We pray that your hearts will remain soft as you read these tales of loss, bravery, struggle and injustice. As Dr. McKenzie writes in A Little Book for New Historians, “We can’t study the past for long without encountering individuals who did or said or believed things that we now hold to be immoral, even evil. And when that happens, our hearts and minds will lead us down one of two paths: toward self-exaltation-”God, I thank you that I am not like other people”--or toward a deeper awareness of our need for grace- “God, have mercy on me, a sinner.”

➜ READ
Page 1-19 “I am a Man”

➜ NARRATE
Narrate orally or in writing to yourself or others.

➜ WRITE
Answer the questions in three paragraphs: 
What shocked you about the reading? What was new to you? What have you encountered before?

➜ MAP WORK
Use the map template to track where the story goes. What are the states Starita mentions throughout the chapter? Where is the Ponca tribe located?

terms.content:
Ancient History: Grades 10-12
· In what ways does the Roman Republic remind you of your own country? In what ways is it different?

·  The Aeneid: Describe the character of Aeneas. What do you think are his defining traits? Give a few concrete examples of where you see them in the Aeneid. 

· Draw two pieces of art that you remember seeing this term. Briefly say what they are and describe them. What do you think they say about the cultures that they were a part of?

· Ancient Empires: What does the IEMP model use for an approach to studying Ancient History?

Ancient History: Grades 10-12
· Based on your readings in Ancient Empires this term, how do you think that the Roman Empire was able to hold together such a large group of people? How was the empire different from other empires you have studied, or from the Roman Republic that preceded it?

·  Choose one:
- In book six of the Aeneid, Vergil describes Aeneas’ journey into the underworld. How does he describe the underworld? What does it tell you about the Roman view of the afterlife and how is that description similar or different from your beliefs about life after death?
- Draw two pieces of art that you remember seeing this term. Briefly say what they are and describe them. What do you think they say about the cultures that they were a part of? 
- Early Christian Writings & "Martyrdom of Ignatius" online resource: Compare the martyrdom stories that you read of Polycarp and Ignatius of Antioch. What do they have in common? How are they different? What character traits do both men share?

Ancient History: Grades 10-12
·  Choose one: 
- The most common adjective that Vergil uses to describe Aeneas is “pious.” Is this the adjective you would have used to describe his character? From the books of the Aeneid you have read, tell what ways this piety is exemplified and why you think this was important to Vergil.
- Early Christian Writings: Based on the writings of the early church fathers that you have read this term, what were some of the primary concerns of the early church? What are the most common themes? Are the concerns of the early church the same as or different than the concerns of the Christian church today?
- Describe some of the accusations that the Romans made against the early Christians and how they are clarified or refuted by the apologist Justin Martyr.
- Ancient Empires: How did Diocletian and Constantine help to resolve the crises of the 3rd century? What role did Christianity play in both creating and resolving some of the problems in the late Roman Empire?
